coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive label-free enantioselective quantification of d-/l-leucine enantiomers with a novel detection mechanism using an ultra-small high-quantum yield N-doped CDs prepared by a novel highly fast solvent-free method
coredata.dc:creator: Hormozi Jangi, Saeed Reza
Akhond, Morteza
coredata.dc:description: The key factor for enantioselective sensing is the design of label-free nanoprobes with high enantioselectivity, sensitivity, simplicity, and cost-efficiency. Herein, a novel, ultra-small (0.6 ± 0.2 nm), high-quantum yield (QY = 95.3 %) enantioselective N-doped CDs with too better figures of merit than reported ones was synthesized via a highly fast (synthesis time: 50 s), ultra-simple, and solvent-free method without needing any autoclave, laboratory oven, reflux system, and microwave, or ultrasonic radiation using l-glutamic acid as both single precursor and enantioselective agent. The CDs were characterized for size, morphology, structure, optical properties, and enantioselectivity. Besides, for the first time, a label-free spectrofluorimetric nanosensor was developed for the enantioselective determination of d-/l-leucine in human blood and sports supplements. Two dynamic working ranges of 0.0−1140 nM and 25.0–380.0 nM were obtained for d-leucine (LOD = 1.7 nM) and l-leucine (LOD = 20.0 nM), respectively. The method showed excellent repeatability and reproducibility along with ultra-sensitivity towards d-leucine (11.7-fold higher than l-leucine). Besides, the nanosensor exhibited excellent selectivity for leucine quantification against interferences (even, isoleucine). Finally, the mechanistic studies were performed for providing the rational reasons behind the label-free enantioselectivity of the as-prepared CDs, revealing a novel mechanism of nanosensor. The nanosensor showed better figures of merit than reported works.

(A) The plots of log (F0-F)/F vs. log[Q] for calculation of the number of binding sites and binding constants of d- and l-leucine. Experimental conditions: d-Leu, 3.8 × 10−9-5.0 × 10-8 M; l-Leu, 2.5 × 10-8-3.8 × 10-7 M; phosphate buffer (pH 7.6), 0.05 M; CDs, 3.30 mg mL-1; wavelength, 454.0 nm and (B) proposed mechanism of the developed sensor for label-free enantioselective determination of d-/l-leucine.

Experimental conditions: phosphate buffer (pH, 7.6), 0.05 M; CDs, 3.30 mg mL−1; wavelength, 454.0 nm.

(A) Selectivity of the developed sensor for leucine determination. Experimental conditions: phosphate buffer (pH, 7.6), 0.05 M; CDs, 3.30 mg mL−1; d-and l-leucine, 38.0 nM; isoleucine, 340.0 nM; arginine, 2000.0 nM; asparagine monohydrate, 3810.0 nM; tyrosine, 350.0 nM; cysteine, 500.0 nM; glutamine, 300.0 nM; histidine, 1000.0 nM; glutamic acid, 2080.0 nM; serine, 2000.0 nM; phenylalanine, 1000.0 nM; tryptophan, 500.0 nM; alanine, 2800.0 nM; valine, 470.0 nM; Fe(II), 2000.0 nM; Cu(II), 1500.0 nM; NaCl, 2500.0 nM; KCl, 2500.0 nM; wavelength, 454.0 nm. and (B) Calibration plot for different mole fractions of d-leucine in the mixtures with its enantiomeric isomer at a fixed total leucine concentration of 300.0 nM; phosphate buffer (pH 7.6), 0.05 M; CDs, 3.30 mg mL−1; wavelength, 454.0 nm.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive label-free enantioselective quantification of d-/l-leucine enantiomers with a novel detection mechanism using an ultra-small high-quantum yield N-doped CDs prepared by a novel highly fast solvent-free method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 71454, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Prof. Massumi Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The key factor for enantioselective sensing is the design of label-free nanoprobes with high enantioselectivity, sensitivity, simplicity, and cost-efficiency. Herein, a novel, ultra-small (0.6 ± 0.2 nm), high-quantum yield (QY = 95.3 %) enantioselective N-doped CDs with too better figures of merit than reported ones was synthesized via a highly fast (synthesis time: 50 s), ultra-simple, and solvent-free method without needing any autoclave, laboratory oven, reflux system, and microwave, or ultrasonic radiation using l-glutamic acid as both single precursor and enantioselective agent. The CDs were characterized for size, morphology, structure, optical properties, and enantioselectivity. Besides, for the first time, a label-free spectrofluorimetric nanosensor was developed for the enantioselective determination of d-/l-leucine in human blood and sports supplements. Two dynamic working ranges of 0.0−1140 nM and 25.0–380.0 nM were obtained for d-leucine (LOD = 1.7 nM) and l-leucine (LOD = 20.0 nM), respectively. The method showed excellent repeatability and reproducibility along with ultra-sensitivity towards d-leucine (11.7-fold higher than l-leucine). Besides, the nanosensor exhibited excellent selectivity for leucine quantification against interferences (even, isoleucine). Finally, the mechanistic studies were performed for providing the rational reasons behind the label-free enantioselectivity of the as-prepared CDs, revealing a novel mechanism of nanosensor. The nanosensor showed better figures of merit than reported works.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an important group of green nanomaterials with strong fluorescence, emission-color-tuning, non-toxic elements, and biocompatibility [1,2]. The carbon dots were accidentally discovered and worked up as the co-product of the single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) during their electrophoretic purification [1,2]. In this case, the reaction mixture contained SWCNTs and a mixture of a fluorescent nanomaterial as the impurity, i.e., raw CDs. These impurities exhibited a size-dependent tunable emission (yellow, green-blue, and orange) under UV irradiation (365 nm). The experimental characterization revealed that a redshift of the maximum fluorescence wavelength of the discovered CDs was occurring with increasing their particle size [3] and their luminescence properties are characteristically dependent on their surface defects [4]. Recently, element-doped CDs, for instance, N-, S-, Si-, and Co-doped CDs or their corresponding co-doped CDs [5–8] have been synthesized and characterized through the different routes, for instance, electrochemical exfoliation of graphite, hydrothermal carbonization, laser ablation, acid dehydration, as well as microwave or ultrasonic based methods [1]. Due to the characteristic advantages of CDs, they have been used for both analytical and bioanalytical purposes, for example, pH sensing and metal ions or molecular substances detection [9–13]. Moreover, fluorescent nanomaterials are very prominent in the field of enantioselective nano- and biosensors for both clinical aims and quality control due to their low-cost, simplicity, selectivity, and sensitivity [14–19,74–76]. Regarding these sensors, enantioselective sensing and biosensing using functionalized CDs are very glaring due to the above-mentioned significant advantages of CDs [20–23].

It is well known that the rapid and accurate determination of amino acids and their chiral recognition is an attractive research field [24–31] due to their characteristic role in the biosynthesis of proteins [32], their characteristic role during pregnancy [33], their role in rheumatoid arthritis [34], needing nitrogen balance for metabolic control [35], serving as energy sources, contributing immune responses linked to metastasis and tumorigenesis [36], also the consideration of some essential amino acids as the biomarkers of the cardiovascular disease including threonine, leucine, isoleucine, and phenylalanine [37]. The most important challenges in this field include: (I) low selectivity of current sensors towards one amino acid vs other amino acids, (II) poor enantioselectivity of current sensors toward an amino acid in the presence of its enantiomeric isomer, and (III) high cost of current high-impact methods such as liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry and capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometry [24–35]. Hence, the key factor is the design of a nanoprobe that can be free from labeling and reveal high enantioselectivity and sensitivity along with simplicity and cost-efficiency. Among the amino acids, essential amino acids such as leucine are the most efficient amino acids considering their vital role in the biosynthesis of proteins, homeostasis of blood glucose, nitrogen balance, and biomarker of cardiovascular disease, and so on. It should be noted that the essential amino acids must be supplied from the diet because the body cannot synthesize them [33,37]. Athletes especially bodybuilders supply essential amino acids such as leucine from the bodybuilding supplements [38–40]. Hence, the sensitive, fast, and accurate enantioselective determination of essential amino acids such as leucine are important for probing cardiovascular disease through its analysis in the bio-samples also for checking the accuracy of its reported amounts in the commercial sports supplements. The current methods for leucine determination are including GC–MS, LC/MS/MS, HPLC/MS/MS, bienzyme biosensors, amperometric bienzyme screen-printed biosensors, ion mobility spectrometry-mass spectrometry, column-switching HPLC, voltammetric sensors (e.g. MWCNT/GCE), and cellulose nanocrystal electrochemical based sensors. However, most of the current protocols have been developed for only l-leucine determination but not for leucine enantioselective quantification [65–73]. Regard the enantioselective determination of leucine, in 2019, cube-shaped gold nanoparticles were introduced as colorimetric probes, however, for this novel report, the measurements only were performed at 5.0 mM, and the calibration plots, detection limits, and selectivity of the probe against other amino acids was not checked [25]. Based on our knowledge, up to now, the spectrofluorimetric sensor for leucine enantioselective determination was not reported in the literature. It is maybe due to the significant common interference of iso-leucine for leucine determination which resulted in poor sensors [66,67,69], it should be mentioned that the leucine determination with good selectivity against isoleucine, prior to detection is a key consideration.

In this contribution, we aimed to design a novel enantioselective CD via an ultra-simple solvent-free method and using it for the label-free d-/L-leucine enantioselective determination. First, novel, ultra-small (0.6 ± 0.2 nm), high-quantum yield (QY = 95.3 %) enantioselective N-doped CD, with better figures of merit than those reported, were synthesized via a highly fast (synthesis time: 50 s), ultra-simple, and solvent-free method without needing any autoclave, laboratory oven, reflux system, and microwave, or ultrasonic radiation using l-glutamic acid as both single precursor and enantioselective agent. The CDs were characterized for size, morphology, structure, optical properties, and enantioselectivity, indicating better figures of merit of these CDs than other reported ones. Then, a label-free nanosensor was developed for the enantioselective determination of d-/L-leucine in human blood and sports supplements. The effective factors on the enantioselective quantification of leucine were optimized and the figures of merits of sensor including calibration plots, detection limits, repeatability, reproducibility, selectivity, quenching mechanism, binding constant, number of binding sites, and real sample analysis (biological samples and sports supplements) were evaluated. Finally, the mechanistic studies were performed for providing the rational reasons behind the label-free enantioselectivity of the as-prepared CDs.

L-glutamic acid, l-leucine, d-leucine, isoleucine, arginine, asparagine monohydrate, tyrosine, cysteine, glutamine, histidine, serine, phenylalanine, tryptophan, alanine, valine, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, FeCl2, Cu(NO3)2, NaCl, KCl, and NaOH were obtained from Merck Company. Deionized water from Zolal Teb Shimico (Iran) Company and commercial bodybuilding supplements such as amino-preload (BCAA) from NPL Company and hyper mass gainer (MASS-VIT) from Vitap Nutrition Company were obtained.

A spectrum RX1 (Perkin Elmer, 940 Winter Street, Waltham, and Massachusetts 02451, USA), an Ultrospec 4000 UV–vis spectrophotometer manufactured by Pharmacia Biotech (Biochrom) Ltd, a MIRA3-TESCAN (15 KV, www.tescan.com) electronic microscope, a transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, model EL10C), a particle size analyzer (PZA, model: SALD-301 V, Shimadzu), and a Perkin-Elmer LS50B spectrofluorometer (Perkin Elmer, 940 Winter Street, Waltham, and Massachusetts 02451, USA) were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent-free synthesis of enantioselective N-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ultra-fast solvent-free synthesis of enantioselective N-doped CDs was performed using a heater stirrer as the heating source and a watch glass as the sample pan without using any autoclave, laboratory oven, reflux system, and microwave, or ultrasonic radiation. l-glutamic acid was used as the enantioselective agent and both carbon and nitrogen sources. For the laboratory-scale synthesis of the N-doped CDs, 200.0 mg L-glutamic acid was placed in a watch glass, then it directly heated at 210 °C using a heater stirrer about 50 s for completing the solvent-free carbonization. The resulted enantioselective N-doped CDs were dissolved in DI water and stored at 4 °C under dark. The synthesis of the introduced CDs was also scaled up using 1.0 g l-glutamic acid at the same operational conditions with the lab-scale synthesis, revealing a carbonization time of about 4.0 min. Scheme S1 shows the schematic representation of the synthesis method and the photographs of the CDs under both visible light and UV lamp.

In a typical experiment, 60.0 μL phosphate buffer (0.05 M; pH, 7.6), 60.0 μL N-doped CDs (final concentration, 3.3 mg mL−1), and 200.0 μL of d-/L-leucine (different concentrations) were added in order to a sample tube. Then the mixture was diluted to its final volume (2.0 mL) with DI water and the fluorescence spectra were recorded over 400−600 nm (Ex, 350 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application and real sample analysis

ce:section.ce:para: The l-leucine content of seven different human blood serum samples was analyzed by the developed sensor for both its original concentration and upon spiked-recovery experiments. Regarding the analysis with the presented sensor, first, the serum samples were diluted 650-fold, then, analyzed for their original amount of l-leucine. Moreover, the d-leucine was determined by the developed method in the presence of l-isomer by spiked-recovery method upon addition original concentration of d-leucine over 0.0−0.005 μg mL−1 (0.0–38.0 nM) in serum for proving the ability of method for recovery of d-leucine in the presence of its enantiomeric isomer in the real media.

Commercial bodybuilding supplements were used for real sample analysis. In the first step, the BCAA tablets (27 mg leucine per 2 tablets) from NPL Company were analyzed. To do this, the tablets were weighed, powdered, and a solution with the final leucine concentration of 13.5 mg L−1 (100 μM) was prepared via dissolving one powdered tablet in 1.0 L water. The solution was then diluted 50-fold (0.27 mg L−1 leucine), and 200.0 μL of the resulted solution (final conc. in the test tube, 0.027 mg L−1) was used for the leucine determination using the developed method.

Also, hyper mass gainer (MASS-VIT) from Vitap Nutrition Company was analyzed for its leucine content using the developed method. The leucine content of the MASS-VIT is reported as 1180 mg leucine per 227 g of powder by the Vitap Nutrition Company (i.e., 1.0 g powder contains 5.2 mg leucine). Hence, for the analysis purpose, a solution of leucine with the final concentration of 5.2 mg L−1 (39.0 μM) was prepared via dissolving 1.0 g powder in 1.0 L water. Then, the solution was diluted 20-fold (0.26 mg L−1 leucine), and 200.0 μL of the resulted solution was used (final conc. in the test tube, 0.026 mg L-1) for the leucine determination using the developed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and characterization of as-prepared CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The solvent-free carbonization time was optimized as the most important factor that affected the CDs synthesis. To do this, the synthesis of carbon dots was performed using 200.0 mg L-glutamic acid over 10–80 seconds. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were shown in Fig. S1. As shown in this figure, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was increased by increasing the carbonization time and achieved its maximal value after 50 s and then leveled off.

The optical properties of CDs including absorption and PL behavior, optical bandgaps, FL reproducibility, FL shelf-life, and QY were evaluated by UV–vis and FL spectroscopy. UV–vis spectroscopy was utilized for the investigation of absorption behavior and optical bandgaps of the as-synthesized CDs. The UV–vis spectrum of the CDs is shown in Fig. S2A, Regard this figure, the CDs have a λmax at 230.0 nm in UV and a λmax at 330.0 nm in the visible region which are related to the π-π* transitions of the conjugated CC and n– π* transitions of the CO group of the l-glutamic acid, respectively [41]. Moreover, the optical band gaps of the CDs were estimated using the Tauc method based on the literature [42]. The Tauc plot of CDs is shown in Fig. S2B, as shown in this figure, the CDs have two bandgaps at 2.23 eV and 3.50 eV which are related to n– π* transitions of the CO and π-π* transitions of the CC band, respectively.

Fig. 1 A shows the excitation and emission peaks of the CD as well as its photographs under visible light and UV-lamp (365 nm). Moreover, the emission spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. 1B showed that the fluorescence λmax of the CDs is about 454.0 nm over 320–380.0 nm as the excitation wavelengths and then redshifted at higher excitation wavelengths than 380.0 nm. However, the maximum FL intensity was not constant in the different excitation wavelengths and maximized over 370−380 nm.

The reproducibility of the FL properties of the CDs was checked by FL measurements of six replicate solutions of the CDs (Fig. S3), indicating an RSD of 2.7 %. Moreover, the storage stability (shelf-life) of CDs was evaluated using a solution of 3.3 mg mL−1 of CDs over 1–45 days storage at 4 °C under dark. The corresponding FL spectra and the histogram of FL stability of introduced CDs against storage time are shown in Fig. S4A and Fig.S4B, respectively. As shown in these figures, the FL intensity of the stored CDs was stabled for 15 days and then decreased to 84.7 % of its initial intensity after 45 days of storage. The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was calculated using l-tryptophan as the reference (FL λmax, 370.0 nm; QYR = 0.14; solvent, water, n2 R = 1.0) [43,44] by the following formula; QY = QYR (I/IR) (AR/A) (n2/n2 R) [45]. The results showed that the absolute quantum yield of the CDs was equaled 0.953 or its percentage was 95.3 %. Based on the above investigations, the introduced CDs reveal a high quantum yield and reproducibility as well as good storage stability.

The size and morphology of N-doped CDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image of the as-synthesized N-doped CDs is shown in Fig. 1C indicated that the CDs were spherical particles with a narrow size distribution over 0.5–1.85 nm with an average of 0.85 nm. Moreover, the DLS analysis for more precisely reporting the size distribution of the CDs was performed (Fig. 1D). The analysis showed that the CDs have a size distribution between 0.4–1.0 nm with the mode value of 0.6 nm and an average size of 0.6 ± 0.2 nm that is closed to the average size obtained from the TEM results. Based on these analyses, the introduced CDs have ultra-small particle size and narrow size distribution.

The structural properties of the N-doped CDs were checked by UV–vis, FT-IR, and EDX analysis. The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs was recorded for investigation of its functional groups (Fig. 2 A). The sharp peaks at 3331 cm−1 and 3402 cm−1 are related to the NH2 group of primary amines [46,47]. The peak at 707 cm−1 is related to the NH wang of primary amines present on the CDs surface [48]. Also, the peaks at 1650.0 cm−1 and 1237 cm−1 can be assigned to the CO and CN bonds on the surface of CDs [48]. Moreover, the band at 1720 cm−1 is related to the CO stretch of ketones (L-glutamic acids) [49]. Based on the IR results, the as-synthesized CDs have free NH2 and CO functional groups. These functional groups can be used for enantioselective interactions between the CDs and amino acids enantiomers. The elemental composition of the CDs was checked by the EDX analysis, the EDX spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2B) shows the characteristic peaks of C, N, and O for the CDs. Also, the semi-quantitative results of the EDX analysis are shown in Table S1, based on these results, the typical formula of the introduced CDs was estimated as C189N69O67.

Based on the IR results, the as-synthesized CDs have free NH2 and CO functional groups. These functional groups can be used for the label-free enantioselective interactions between the CDs and amino acid enantiomers. For exploring more precise about chiroptical properties of as-synthesized CDs, their circular dichroism was recorded (Fig. S4C), revealing one negative and one positive Cotton effects at 230 nm and 326 nm, respectively which are assigned to the π-π* and n- π* transitions of l-glutamic acid. Hence, based on this spectrum, it can be concluded that the chiral properties of l-glutamic acid were saved during the synthesis process. Moreover, the circular polarization fluorescence of the enantioselective N-doped CDs was recorded (Fig. S4D), showing a band at about 453.0 nm. Considering the chiral properties of as-synthesized CDs, their enantioselectivity was checked towards 38.0 nM of d-/L-leucine enantiomers in phosphate buffer solution (0.05 M; pH, 7.6) using 3.3 mg mL−1 of CDs. The results are shown in Fig. 3 indicated that the CDs interact more selectively with d-leucine enantiomers than the l-leucine. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of CDs quenches in the presence of l-/D-leucine with a considerable distinction between l- and d-leucine. This is maybe related to the CDs structure that the l-glutamic acid was used for its synthesis, so the CDs interact with the d-enantiomers rather than the l-type due to the stereospecific interactions as previously reported for the l-functional nanoparticles which have a stronger interaction with d-enantiomers [50]. The enantioselectivity coefficient (α= (F0-FD)/(F0-FL) [51]) of the CDs was found to be about 2.3, indicating excellent enantioselectivity of the introduced CDs. It should be mentioned that the rational reasons behind the label-free enantioselectivity of as-prepared CDs and the higher analytical response of d-leucine than its l-isomer were discussed in detail as the possible detection mechanism by both experimental and theoretical validations (see section 3.2).

A typical comparison between figures of merit of as-prepared N-doped CDs and reported ones especially amino-acid-based CDs was performed. The results were shown in Table S2, revealing too better figures of merit of the as-prepared N-doped CDs than reported ones [5, 8. 10, 11, 20–23, 41, 45, 64]. As shown in Table S2, the %QY of amino acids based CDs is as low as 10–12.3 % and their synthesis was performed in the presence of an acid source (citric acid), also regard the antioxidant derived CDs, the %QY is reported about 40 % and their synthesis needs an amine source for efficient N-doping, however, these CDs show no enantioselectivity properties for chiral applications. Moreover, these CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method with a synthesis time as high as 6.0 h.

Besides, the most of other reported CDs show low %QY in the range of 3.0–24.4 % and need long synthesis time over 2.0–80.0 hrs for preparation, however, some microwave-assisted methods showed medium synthesis time between 5.0–15.0 min and acceptable %QY (e.g. 39.5 %). Also, the reported CDs cannot be used as label-free chiral probes and need modification with chiral labels such as l-cysteine, organic frameworks, and β-cyclodextrin for application toward chiral sensing which limits their practical applications. In addition, the size of reported CDs is varied from 2.14 to 14.5 nm while the as-prepared CDs have an ultra-small particle with a size of 0.6 nm, the as-prepared N-doped CDs have a %QY as high as 95.3 % and were synthesized using l-glutamic acid as the single precursor (carbon, nitrogen, and acid sources) and enantioselective agent. Moreover, the synthesis time of the as-prepared CDs is too short (50 s) and they can be applied as label-free enantioselective probes for chiral determination of amino acids without any modification. The synthesis process of as-prepared CDs is a solvent-free method which is a significant advantage for this process compared to the other reported methods, from a green chemistry point of view. Based on these considerations, it can be concluded that the figures of merit of the as-prepared CDs are too better than other reported ones

The mechanistic studies were performed for providing the rational reasons behind the label-free enantioselectivity of as-prepared CDs and the higher analytical response of d-leucine than its l-isomer. It is well known that several factors such as transition type, the effect of conjugation, rigid plane structure, electrostatic interaction, complexation, and special groups, as well as charge transfer can affect the FL behavior and fluorescence intensity of CDs [64]. Based on the FT-IR results, the as-synthesized CDs have free NH2 and CO functional groups. These functional groups can be used for the label-free enantioselective interactions between the CDs and amino acid enantiomers. For testing this proposal, the FL spectra of the CDs in the presence and the absence of d- and l-leucine were recorded (Fig. 3), revealing a significant quenching in the FL intensity of introduced CDs by leucine molecules. The quenching by d-Leu is catachrestically higher than the l-leucine may be due to stronger stereospecific interactions between the introduced CDs and d-enantiomers rather than the l-type. At the first step, it can be proposed that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) via electron transfer from the negatively-charged carboxyl groups of leucine to the positively-charged amino groups of CDs or from the negatively charged carboxyl residues of CDs to the positively charged amino group of leucine is may be responsible for this significant quenching as reported in the literature [20,58,59].

As a significant difference between the mechanism of this method with other reported methods, the energy transfer can simultaneously occur from CDs to leucine and from leucine to CDs because, at the optimal conditions (pH = 7.6), the CDs have both negatively charged carboxyl residues (energy donor) of CDs and positively charged amino residues (energy acceptor) on their surface also, the leucine molecules at these conditions have positive amino- (energy acceptor) and negative carboxyl- (energy donor) groups which can form two Meisenheimer complexes on the surface of CDs per one leucine molecule, not one Meisenheimer complex (reported CDs can form only one Meisenheimer complex per one molecule of quencher [20,58–60]). More precisely, the investigation of the pH effect (Fig. S7) revealed that 7.6 is the best pH for both sensitivity and enantioselectivity of the sensor (see section 3.3). Based on the literature [55,56], in the physiological conditions, the glutamate anion (i.e., −OOC−CH(NH3 +)-(CH2)2−COO−) is the stable form of l-glutamic acid (i.e., the enantioselective agent of CDs). Therefore, at the detection conditions, the electron transfer and electrostatic interactions have occurred from the negatively charged carboxylate groups presented on the surface of CDs to the positively charged amino group of d-leucine also from the carboxylate of d-leucine to the positively charged amino group of CDs resulting in two Meisenheimer complex per one molecule of leucine [60].

For exploring more precise about the interaction between the CDs and d-/L-leucine enantiomers, the zeta potential of the CDs was measured at pH = 7.6 in the presence and the absence of 38.0 nM of d-/L-leucine. The results showed that the native CDs have a zeta potential of -22.3 mV while after interaction with d-and l-leucine, the zeta potential was found to be -6.7 mV and -15.4 mV, respectively. Based on these data, the CDs interact with leucine molecules while their interaction with d-leucine is significantly stronger than that of l-isomer. Also, these data proved the occurring electrostatic interactions during the quenching process.

However, a reliable way for providing the rational reasons behind the enantioselectivity of as-prepared CDs, the higher analytical response of d-leucine than its l-isomer, and proving the possible complexation during the quenching process is considering the number of binding sites (N) and binding constant (K) for the d- and l-leucine. As previously was reported in the literature [18], the equilibrium between bounded molecules (here, leucine-CDs) and free ones (here, CDs or leucine) for the binding of small molecules (here, leucine) to the binding sites presented on the surface of nanoparticles or macromolecules is defined as follow: log (F0-F)/F = log K + N log[Q], where K and N are represented to the binding constant and the number of binding sites per CD particle, respectively. Hence, for more precisely answering this question that why the CDs interacted selectively to d-Leu rather than l-isomer, the values of K and N were calculated for both d- and l-leucine (Fig. 4 A) via plotting the log (F0-F)/F vs. log [D-/L-Leu] which the curve has an intercept of logK and a slope of N. Regard these plots, the K was calculated as 7.1 × 10+4 M−1 and 10.26 M−1 for d- and l-leucine per one CD particle, respectively, with a ratio of KD-Leu/KL-Leu = 6788.6, indicated that the interaction between CDs and d-Leu is 6788.6-fold stronger than that of l-Leu. Moreover, the N value was calculated as 1.23 and 0.46 for d- and l-leucine, respectively. The ratio of the number of binding sites (N) for d- and l-leucine were found to be ND-Leu/NL-Leu = 2.61, indicated that the binding sites are more available for d-Leu than l-Leu. These results also proved the forming complexes between leucine and CDs during the quenching process. Moreover, the ultra-sensitivity of CDs toward d-leucine is clearly explained by the results obtained from the binding constant and site estimation.

Besides, a rational way for evaluation of the strength of the complexation between the d- or l-leucine molecules and the edge groups of as-prepared CDs is the calculation of the binding Gibbs free energy (ΔG) as reported in the literature [20]. Hence, the binding Gibbs free energy (kJ mol−1) for interactions between as-prepared CDs and both d-/L-leucine were calculated using the following formula [61,62]; ΔG= -RTlnK where K, T, and R, are represented to the binding constant per one CDs particle (M−1), temperature (kelvin), and gas constant (kJ mol−1 K−1). Considering the binding constant of d- (7.1 × 10+4 M−1) and l-leucine (10.26 M−1) per one CD particle as well as T = 298.15 K, the ΔG value was calculated as -27.69 kJ mol−1 and -5.77 kJ mol−1 for d- and l-leucine, respectively. As previously reported in the literature, the negative Gibbs free energy value is attributed to the thermodynamically favorable binding between CDs and the quencher (here, d- or l-leucine molecules) during non-ionic interaction [61]. Besides, the more negative ΔG value for d-leucine interactions with CDs indicates the very stronger binding between d-leucine and CDs (more stable complex) during the quenching process than the l-isomer.

In addition, the FRET efficiency (E) can be used for proving that the energy transfer from donor to acceptor can be varied with changing the enantiomeric type of leucine molecules. Hence, the steady-state FRET efficiency was calculated for both d- and l-leucine using the following formula as reported [62,63]; E = 1-F/F0, where F and F0 are represented to the FL intensity of the donor compound in the presence and absence of acceptor. The energy transfer efficiency (%) was calculated as 21.3 % and 8.9 % for d- and l-leucine, respectively. Hence, it can be concluded that the energy transfer efficiency for d-leucine interactions with CDs is too quite higher than that of l-isomer. It is maybe related to different electrostatic polarization resulted from different dipole moments of d- and l-isomers (different in the sign not in the quantity) [63]. The ratio of E for d- and l-leucine is about E(D-Leu)/E(L-Leu) = 2.4 which is in good agreement with the enantioselectivity coefficient of the CDs (α = 2.3). It should be noted that the data of the binding of CDs with d- and l-leucine were summarized in Table S3.

Moreover, it is well known that the possible complexation between leucine and CDs during the quenching process can be validated by investigation of the FL quenching mechanism. As previously were reported in the literature [57], the quenching of the FL intensity of a fluorescence probe occurs through a dynamic or static pathway. The effect of temperature on the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot can be used as an index for distinguishing between these quenching pathways. If the quenching follows a dynamic mechanism, the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot will increase by increasing the temperature. However, in the case of a static mechanism, the change in the reaction temperature does not affect the slope [57]. The mechanism of possible interaction between leucine molecules and CDs was investigated by plotting the Stern-Volmer plots at different temperatures (Fig. S5). The results show that the increase in the reaction temperature has no considerable effect on the slope of the curves, indicating a possible static pathway for the quenching of FL intensity of CDs in the presence of leucine enantiomers. The static pathway of the fluorescence quenching of the introduced CDs by the leucine molecules (Fig. S5) points to form a complex between the leucine and introduced CDs during the quenching process [60]. Hence, based on these validations, a typical possible interaction between the CDs and leucine and consequently the possible mechanism of the detection system was schematized as shown in Fig. 4B. As shown in Fig. 4B, the as-prepared CDs can simultaneously act as the electron donor for energy transfer to positively charged amino-groups of leucine molecules to form a CDs-Leu complex and as the electron acceptor for accepting energy from the negatively charged carboxyl groups of leucine molecules to form a Leu-CDs complex, resulting in the ultra-sensitivity against d-enantiomer (11.7-fold higher than l-isomer). It is notable that the difference in binding behavior of l- and d-leucine with the developed l-glutamic acid-based CDs is assigned to the differences in their spatial properties. More precisely, the interactions between amino acids are including hetero-chiral and homo-chiral interactions, however, the hetero-chiral interactions are stronger and more preferred than homo-chiral interactions. In the case of this sensor, the positively charged amino groups of l-Leu and l-glutamic acid-based CDs have the same spatial orientation, leading to strong repulsion and consequently weak homo-chiral interactions (binding constant = 10.26 M−1) between l-Leu molecules and l-glutamic acid-based CDs, resulted in low FL quenching. While the repulsions between d-Leu and l-glutamic acid-based CDs (with opposite spatial orientations) are in their minimal values, causing strong hetero-chiral interactions (binding constant = 7.1 × 10+4 M-1) and consequently high FL quenching. Hence, when the l-glutamic acid is used as the enantioselective agent, the resulted CDs showed stronger interactions with d-leucine, and so ultra-sensitivity toward d-enantiomer. However, it can be accepted that if d-glutamic acid is used as a single precursor instead of l-glutamic acid, the sensitivity toward l-leucine will enhance and the d-glutamic acid-based CDs can be used for the construction of a possible ultra-sensitive sensor toward l-leucine [50]. Overall, in this novel mechanism, two Meisenheimer complexes per one molecule of quencher were formed unlike the mechanism of other reported works which only one Meisenheimer complexes per one molecule of quencher formed during their detection (i.e., those CDs could act only as an electron donor or acceptor) [20,58–60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of a label-free enantioselective sensor for d-/l-leucine
ce:section.ce:para: Factors affecting the label-free enantioselective determination of d-/L-leucine, including the concentration of N-doped CDs, pH, buffer concentration, and response time were optimized concerning Δ(F0/F)=[(F0/F) d-Leu]-[(F0/F) l-Leu] as the analytical signal. In this regard, the concentration of N-doped CDs on its enantioselectivity was checked over 2.2–5.5 mg mL−1 at PBS (0.1 M; pH, 7.6) in the presence of 50 nM of d- or l-leucine. The corresponding FL spectra, as well as the plots of F0/F vs. CDs concentration and Δ(F0/F) vs. CDs concentration, are shown in Fig. S6A-C. As shown in this figure, the CDs performances for label-free enantioselective detection of leucine (i.e., the distinction between l- and d-leucine) reached its maximum value at the 3.3 mg mL−1 of CDs and then decreased by increasing the final concentration of CDs.

Also, the effect of pH on the enantioselectivity of CDs was checked at PBS (0.1 M) over a pH range of 5.0–9.0 in the presence of 50 nM of d- or l-leucine using 3.3 mg mL−1 CDs as both fluorescence probe and enantioselector. The results are represented in Fig. S7A-C, showing an improvement in the enantioselectivity of CDs by increasing pH from the acidic to natural conditions with maximum performance at physiological pH (pH, 7.6). It should be mentioned that the Δ(F0/F) value at the alkaline media is approximately the same as the acidic media. This behavior is maybe explained by the following statements. The l-glutamic acid was used as both nitrogen and carbon source as well as the enantioselective agent for the analytical probe (CDs).

The l-glutamic acid with the ideal molecular structure of HOOC−CH(NH2)-(CH2)2−COOH has two carboxyl groups and one amino group (Fig. S8). In the soluble form, its amino group protonates or its carboxyl groups deprotonate in different pHs. Based on the literature, at lower pH than 2.5, the glutamic acid is at its positive protonated form (i.e., HOOC−CH(NH3 +)-(CH2)2−COOH) [52], while at higher pH than 2.5, the neutral zwitterion form of glutamic acid produces (i.e., −OOC−CH(NH3 +)-(CH2)2−COOH) [53,54]. Also, it is reported that at alkaline media, the doubly-negative anion of glutamic acid produces (i.e., −OOC−CH(NH2)-(CH2)2−COO−) [55]. However, in physiological conditions, the glutamate anion is the stable form of glutamic acid (i.e., −OOC−CH(NH3 +)-(CH2)2−COO−) [55,56].

According to the above discussion, at the acidic media, the interactions between the leucine enantiomers and CDs were occurred through the neutral zwitterion form of glutamic acid (i.e., one carboxylic acid and amino group) and at the alkaline media through its doubly-negative anion. However, in the physiological pH, the electrostatic interactions occurred between the leucine molecules and CDs through the amino, and both carboxylic acid residues of CDs. It means that, in these conditions, the electron transfer was occurred from negatively charged carboxylate residues of the CDs to the amino group of d-leucine also from the negatively charged carboxylate of d-leucine to positively charged amino group of CDs. Hence, the stereospecific interactions have occurred on their maximum level between the CDs and d-leucine at physiological conditions, consequently, the F0/F value increases for both enantiomers due to stronger interactions between CDs and leucine molecules than both acidic and alkaline media, also, the Δ(F0/F) value increases due to the stronger stereospecific interactions between CDs and d-leucine than l-enantiomer.

Additionally, the effect of buffer concentration was checked on the F0/F and Δ(F0/F) values at PBS (pH, 7.6) over 0.01−0.25 M. The corresponding spectra and the resulted plots of the analytical signal and enantioselectivity performances of CDs were shown in Fig. S9A-C. Concerning this figure, both analytical signal (F0/F) and enantioselectivity performances (Δ(F0/F) value) reached their maximum level, when the PBS concentration was fixed at 0.05 M. Moreover, the effect of response time was checked, indicated that the quenching in the FL intensity of CDs has immediately occurred upon the addition of d-/L-leucine enantiomers and fixes for a long time. Hence, future experiments for the label-free enantioselective determination of leucine enantiomers were performed at PBS (0.05 M; pH, 7.6) using 3.3 mg mL−1 CDs as both analytical probe and enantioselector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical figures of merit of the label-free sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical figures of merit of the developed label-free sensor including calibration plots, detection limits, repeatability, and reproducibility (inter- and intra-day assays) were evaluated. The calibration plots for each enantiomer were constructed by plotting F0/F against the d-/L- leucine concentrations. The FL spectra of CDs in the presence of a different concentration of d- and l-leucine were shown in Fig. 5 A and Fig. 5B, respectively, showed that the d-enantiomer interacts with the CDs more effectively than the l-type. The corresponding Stern-Volmer plots for the d-/L-leucine are shown in Fig. 5C. Regarding this figure, two linear ranges of 0.0−50 nM and 50−1140 nM for d-leucine and a linear range of 25.0–380.0 nM for l-leucine were achieved. The calibration sensitivity was calculated as 0.007 and 0.0006 L μM−1 for d- and l-leucine, respectively. Hence, the calibration sensitivity for the d-leucine is about 11.7-fold higher than l-leucine, indicating ultra-sensitivity towards d-leucine. The detection limit of the method was calculated as 1.7 nM for d- and 20.0 nM for l-leucine based on the 3 s law, i.e., 3(standard deviation of blank)/(slope of calibration plot).

Moreover, repeatability and reproducibility of the method were checked for 50.0 nM of d-/L-leucine. Fig.S10 shows the corresponding FL spectra and histogram of founded leucine concentration vs the number of runs. As seen the leucine concentration was determined with no significant changes for the different operational runs. More princely, the %RSD as a measure of repeatability was calculated as 1.2 % and 1.6 % for d- and l-leucine, respectively, indicating good repeatability for the leucine determination. Also, the reproducibility of the method was investigated for both intra- (Fig.S11A) and inter-day (Fig. S11B) assays for 50.0 nM of d-leucine. The %RSD was estimated as 1.4 % and 2.5 % for intra- and inter-day measurements, showing a very good reproducibility for the determination of leucine.

The selectivity of the sensor was checked by determination of the sensor response for 38.0 nM of d- and l-leucine, 340.0 nM of isoleucine, 2000.0 nM of arginine, 3810.0 nM of asparagine monohydrate, 350.0 nM of tyrosine, 500.0 nM of cysteine, 300.0 nM of glutamine, 1000.0 nM of histidine, 2080.0 nM of glutamic acid, 2000.0 nM of serine, 1000.0 nM of phenylalanine, 500.0 nM of tryptophan, 2800.0 nM of alanine, 470.0 nM of valine, 2000.0 nM of Fe(II), 1500.0 nM of Cu(II), 2500.0 nM of NaCl, and 2500.0 nM of KCl. The results are shown in Fig. 6 A, as shown in this figure, the other amino acids, co-existing metal cations, and salts have no significant effect on the FL intensity of the introduced N-doped CDs while leucine causes a considerable quenching in their FL intensity. It should be noted that even isoleucine shows no interference in the determination of leucine. When the molar concentration of the isoleucine in the test tube is 9.0-fold higher than d-leucine, it affects the response of d-leucine by only 0.5 % which this effect is not significant. Moreover, the interaction between CDs with d-leucine is stronger than l-isomer, hence, the analytical response of d-leucine is higher than its l-isomer. Hence, the developed method is appropriate for the chiral recognition of leucine enantiomers and its selective determination of leucine in the presence of other amino acids (even its isomer, isoleucine), salts, and metal cations.

To evaluate the performance of the developed sensor for enantioselective determination of leucine in the presence of other amino acids, 0.005 μg mL−1 d-leucine was measured in the presence and absence of an amino acid mixture including 0.025 μg mL-1 isoleucine, cysteine, asparagine monohydrate, glutamic acid, and glutamic acid. The d-leucine was recovered about 99.7 % and 97.5 % in the absence and presence of the above-mentioned amino acid mixture, revealing selective recovery of leucine in the mixed samples with other amino acids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identifying d/l-leucine in a racemic mixture
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the developed label-free enantioselective sensor for the identification of d-/L-leucine in the presence of its enantiomeric isomer was evaluated by adjusting the enantiomeric percentage. In this regard, the calibration of the mixture of enantiomers was plotted using the determination of F0/F for the different mole ratios of d-leucine in the presence of its corresponding enantiomeric isomer. The total molar concentration of leucine was fixed at 300 nM in all test tubes. The results are shown in Fig. 6B indicated that the method shows higher chiral sensitivity for d-leucine determination than its corresponding l-isomer. The good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9785) between the analytical signal and mole fraction of enantiomers reveals that the method is appropriate for the enantioselective determination of each enantiomer in the presence of its corresponding isomer [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: First, the l-leucine content of human blood serum was determined for both its original concentration and spiked-recovery experiments using the developed method (Table 1 ), achieving good recoveries over 95.0–103.3 %, showing the power of the developed method for the l-leucine analysis in bio-samples. Besides, the recovered original concentration of serum l-leucine using the developed method is completely closed to the l-leucine concentration range reported in the literature [37], revealing high accuracy and reliability of the method for the l-leucine analysis. It should be noted that the concentration of l-leucine increases in the blood from its normal level (24.0 ± 5.1 μg mL−1; 183.0 μM) to the abnormal level (31.0 ± 16.1 μg mL−1; 236.3 μM) in the cardiovascular disease [37], hence, the authors strongly recommended the use of the developed sensor in the cardiovascular patients. Besides, the d-leucine was determined by the developed method in the presence of l-isomer by spiked-recovery experiments for proving the ability of the method for recovery of d-leucine in the presence of its enantiomeric isomer (Table 1). The results showed a good recovery for d-leucine determination over 96.7–100.0 %, indicated that the method is appropriate for d-Leu determination in the presence of its l-isomer.

Moreover, the commercial bodybuilding supplements, including, BCAA tablets from NPL and MASS-VIT powder from Vitap Nutrition Company were analyzed for their leucine content. The results are shown in Table 2 . The good recoveries over 96.3–107.4 % for the determination of leucine content of BCAA tablets and 96.1–100 % for the quantification of leucine MASS-VIT powders were achieved, revealed that the method is appropriate for the analysis of commercial leucine supplements. It should be noted that the calibration sensitivities were the same for both serum and non-serum samples, however, the analytical sensitivity for analysis of non-serum samples (i.e., BCAA tablets) is slightly lower than that for serum samples.

Herein, novel, ultra-small (0.6 ± 0.2 nm), high-quantum yield (QY = 95.3 %) enantioselective N-doped CDs were synthesized via a highly fast (synthesis time: 50 s), ultra-simple, and solvent-free method. The CDs were characterized for size, morphology, structure, optical properties, and enantioselectivity, indicating better figures of merit than reported ones [[5,8,10,11,20–23,41,45,64]. Besides, a label-free nanosensor was developed for the ultra-sensitive enantioselective determination of d-/l-leucine in human blood and sports supplements. Dynamic working ranges of 0.0−1140 nM and 25.0–380.0 nM were obtained for d-leucine (LOD = 1.7 nM) and l-leucine (LOD = 20.0 nM), respectively. In comparison with other reported methods for leucine determination, the developed nanosensor showed better figures of merit (Table S4) [25,65–73]. As shown in Table S4, the LOD of the sensor for both d- and l-leucine is also lower than the other methods. Moreover, most of the reported methods are not enantioselective methods and show high %RSD (3.0–20 %), indicating undesirable repeatability. For the reported enantioselective sensors, the selectivity coefficients for the isomers are usually very poor which limits their practical applications. The %RSD of the developed nanosensor is very low, 1.4 %, revealing its excellent reproducibility. Besides, the method showed ultra-sensitivity towards d-leucine (11.7-fold higher than l-leucine) and excellent selectivity for leucine quantification against interferences (even, isoleucine). Finally, the method was successfully applied for the enantioselective determination of the d-/l-leucine content of human blood and sports supplements. Besides, mechanistic studies were performed for providing the rational reasons behind the label-free enantioselectivity of as-prepared CDs, revealing a novel mechanism of this nanosensor compared to other works.
Saeed Reza Hormozi Jangi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing. Morteza Akhond: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Conceptualization, Review & editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Saeed Reza Hormozi Jangi is a Ph.D. candidate of analytical chemistry in Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. He focusses on developing optical nanosensors such as colorimetric nanozyme-based sensors and fluorescence-based nanoprobes. Currently, he works on construction of optical enantioselective nanosensors for determination of biomaterials and drugs in biological or commercial samples. In his research interests, he also focusses on enzyme immobilization protocols toward biocatalysis and environmental applications.

Morteza Akhond is an associate professor of analytical chemistry at Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. He received his M.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees in analytical chemistry from Shiraz University in 1987 and 1996, respectively. His current research interests are extraction, transport, electrochemistry, spectrophotometry, electrochemical as well as optical sensing, and separations based on nanoparticles.
